utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality audit report than wo_skills. The key differentiators are: (1) J2 — wi_skills explicitly identifies retraining-only as an FDA red flag with a precise 21 CFR 820.100 citation applied to the corrective action inadequacy, while wo_skills only labels it 'weak' without the regulatory red-flag framing; (2) J3 — wi_skills proposes a specific measurable effectiveness criterion (e.g., zero defects in next N releases, test pass rates), while wo_skills only recommends a longer time window (6 months), which remains a passive complaint-based metric. The wi_skills report is more regulatory-precise, uses stronger language ('SYMPTOM,' 'DO NOT SUBMIT TO FDA'), and demonstrates the depth expected from specialized FDA auditing knowledge. The wo_skills report, while competent and structured, lacks the regulatory sharpness needed for actual FDA submission preparation, particularly in flagging training-only CAPAs and proposing measurable effectiveness criteria.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality gap analysis reports that passed all verifier checks and addressed all seven evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills produces a notably superior output: it is larger (16,616 bytes vs 10,453 bytes), identifies 17 Major and 8 Minor findings versus 8 critical gaps, provides deeper regulatory citation depth (e.g., explicitly naming IEC 62304 subsections like 5.4, 5.5.5, 5.6.7, 5.7.4 for reclassification implications), more explicitly articulates the process-description-vs-objective-evidence distinction required by J2, includes a compliance matrix with percentage scores for visual prioritization, and flags the likely Class C misclassification more assertively. The wo_skills report is competent and covers the same high-level gaps but with less regulatory depth and specificity. In this particular test case, both runs score identically on the binary evaluation criteria because the wo_skills agent happened to produce a substantively correct report despite lacking the skill — a borderline outcome that the test scenario anticipated as possible but not expected. Overall, wi_skills is the higher quality output due to greater regulatory depth, specificity of citations, and richer remediation guidance.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced comprehensive audit reports that passed all seven evaluation criteria. Both reports identify the IQ/OQ/PQ structure gap, the 21 CFR 11.10(e) audit trail scope deficiency, the e-signature screenshot as insufficient evidence under 11.50/11.70, the missing predicate rule scoping analysis, the SOC 2 non-substitution principle under ISO 13485, risk stratification for inspection readiness, and the deferred PQ as a current compliance gap. Both are inspection-ready quality outputs. Overall, the two runs are effectively equivalent in regulatory coverage; wi_skills shows marginally better structure and conciseness consistent with skill-augmented regulatory expertise, but both meet all evaluation criteria.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality report. Both runs correctly identified most compliance gaps and produced inspection-ready audit reports. However, wi_skills distinctly outperforms wo_skills on J4 (predicate rule scoping analysis): the skill-equipped agent explicitly identified the missing predicate rule analysis as a compliance gap requiring record-by-record Part 11 applicability determination, while the baseline agent entirely missed this gap. The wi_skills report is also more precise on regulatory citations (explicit 11.10(e) for audit trail, 11.50/11.70 for signatures, 820.70(i) for validation), more detailed on why a screenshot is insufficient as compliance evidence, and better structured for inspection defense with explicit 483-observation risk framing. utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed successfully and produced output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality gap analysis reports that passed all verifier checks and addressed all seven evaluation criteria. Contrary to the test design rationale's prediction that the baseline agent would perform a mere structural checklist review, the wo_skills agent also identified all key compliance gaps including the unsubstantiated safety classification, inadequate verification evidence, missing traceability matrix, non-compliant design review documentation, missing anomaly records, and insufficient risk management file integration. Both reports organized findings by severity and provided submission-readiness prioritization. The wi_skills report (32,729 bytes) is slightly more concise while the wo_skills report (41,198 bytes) is more verbose. The wi_skills report, informed by the regulatory skill, demonstrates deeper regulatory citation precision (e.g., citing IEC 62304 §4.3 specifically for classification, referencing the intended use issue for AF detection suggesting Class C may apply), while the wo_skills report also shows strong regulatory depth by independently identifying cybersecurity documentation gaps and SOUP analysis deficiencies. Overall, both agents performed at a high level on this task, with wi_skills having a marginal edge in regulatory citation specificity and focused depth, while wo_skills compensates with broader scope coverage. The quality difference is minimal for the specific evaluation items assessed.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. The wo_skills run failed verifier check V2 (no explicit regulatory citation found), which is consistent with the absence of 21 CFR 820.100 or other citations in the output.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The key differentiator is regulatory precision: the wi_skills report explicitly cites 21 CFR 820.100(a)(2) and ISO 14971 to ground its severity classifications as 'Major,' flags retraining-only as an FDA red flag with specific statutory citation, and uses standard audit severity terminology (Major/Minor/OFI). The wo_skills report is substantively strong — it correctly identifies the root cause as insufficient, flags the effectiveness check, and notes the risk file gap — but it uses non-standard severity labels ('Critical' instead of 'Major'), omits all regulatory citations, and therefore fails J2 and J5. For actual FDA submission preparation, the wi_skills output is immediately actionable by a QA team, while the wo_skills output would require regulatory citation enrichment before use. Final scores: wi_skills 6/6, wo_skills 4/6.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills report scores 7/7 on all evaluation items, while wo_skills scores only 4/7. The key differentiators are: (1) J2 - wi_skills explicitly cites 21 CFR 11.10(e) and identifies the login/logout-only audit trail as a record-level gap; wo_skills does not. (2) J3 - wi_skills explicitly cites 21 CFR 11.50 and 11.70 and flags the screenshot as inadequate evidence; wo_skills does not. (3) J4 - wi_skills performs a predicate rule scoping analysis and identifies the missing applicability determination; wo_skills entirely omits this. (4) J5 - wi_skills explicitly addresses ISO 13485 Section 4.1.6 and the SOC 2 report inadequacy; wo_skills entirely omits this. The skill-equipped agent demonstrates specialized regulatory knowledge by citing specific subsections, identifying the precise nature of each gap (e.g., session-level vs. record-level audit trails), and applying ISO 13485 requirements — all of which are hallmarks of a regulatory specialist review rather than a general IT documentation review. The wo_skills output, while competent at identifying the PQ gap and general validation deficiencies, lacks the depth needed for inspection readiness and would leave the company with false confidence on audit trail scope, electronic signature compliance, predicate rule scoping, and ISO 13485 obligations.
